---
title: "Introduction"
author: "Truong Phi"
---

## Overview

The **Positional Burrows-Wheeler Transform (PBWT)**, introduced by [Richard Durbin in 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3998136/), revolutionized how we handle massive genomic datasets. By applying the principles of the Burrows-Wheeler Transform to positional genetic data, it enables ultra-efficient haplotype matching and search operations that were previously computationally prohibitive.

The PBWT provides an efficient way to sort haplotypes at every position along a chromosome, enabling very fast identification of identical-by-descent (IBD) segments and other types of haplotype matching.

### Motivation

PBWT algorithms is powerfull but still hard to understand for newbie. Thank for the intensive [Miles's blog](https://alimanfoo.github.io/2016/06/21/understanding-pbwt.html) on creating practical code from the Richard's article, and implementation. However, there is still a missing of intuition of processing, and how it is implemented in real code. I created this blog to give mental models on how PBWT algorithms actually worked by reading [Richard's source code](https://github.com/richarddurbin/pbwt), create pseudo-code (simplify and capture algorithm only), give understanding workflow figures on specific example.  

### Content covered

*   **Construction:** How to build prefix and divergence arrays ([Algorithms 1&2](./pbwt-core/algorithm_12.qmd)).
*   **Internal Matching:** Identifying shared segments and maximal matches within a population:
    - [Algorithms 2&3](./pbwt-core/algorithm_23.qmd): Report matches longer than $L$
    - [Algorithms 2&4](./pbwt-core/algorithm_24.qmd): Report set maximal matches
*   **External Querying:** Mapping a new haplotype against a PBWT database ([Algorithm 2&5](./pbwt-core/algorithm_25.qmd)).



---

::: {#refs}
:::
